This paper introduces a mixed reality-based simulation framework that relies on the integration of the Robot Operating System (ROS) and Unity 3D. The proposed framework allows for the simulation of mobile delivery robots in complex urban environments, combining realistic physics and graphics with actual robot software. We demonstrate the utility of the framework through a series of experiments, showing that it provides a high-fidelity environment for evaluating robot navigation and interaction behaviors in a variety of scenarios.

Title: An ROS-based Shared Communication Middleware for Plug & Play Modular Intelligent Design of Smart Systems.
Authors: Tathagata Chakraborti & Siddharth Srivastava & Alessandro Pinto & Subbarao Kambhampati
Abstract: This paper presents a shared communication middleware based on the Robot Operating System (ROS) for the design of smart systems. The proposed architecture allows for plug-and-play modularity, enabling different components of a smart system—such as sensors, actuators, and intelligent agents—to communicate seamlessly regardless of their underlying hardware or software platforms. We describe the design principles of the middleware, which include a centralized database for state management and a set of standardized interfaces for common smart system tasks.

Title: ROSbag-based Multimodal Affective Dataset for Emotional and Cognitive States.
Authors: Wonse Jo & Shyam Sundar Kannan & Go-Eum Cha & Ahreum Lee & Byung-Cheol Min
Abstract: This paper presents a multimodal affective dataset specifically designed for research in emotional and cognitive state recognition in human-robot interaction. The dataset is provided in the ROSbag format, facilitating its use within the Robot Operating System (ROS) ecosystem. It includes synchronized data from various sensors, such as cameras, microphones, and physiological sensors, captured during interactions between humans and robots. We describe the data collection protocol and provide an initial analysis of the dataset, highlighting its potential for developing and benchmarking affective computing algorithms for social robots.

Title: A ROS-based Framework for Monitoring Human and Robot Conditions in a Human-Multi-robot Team.
Authors: Wonse Jo & Shyam Sundar Kannan & Go-Eum Cha & Ahreum Lee & Byung-Cheol Min
Abstract: This paper presents a framework for monitoring human and robot conditions in human multi-robot interactions. The proposed framework consists of four modules: 1) human and robot conditions monitoring interface, 2) synchronization time filter, 3) data feature extraction interface, and 4) condition monitoring interface. The framework is based on Robot Operating System (ROS), and it supports physiological and behavioral sensors and devices and robot systems, as well as custom programs. Furthermore, it allows synchronizing the monitoring conditions and sharing them simultaneously. In order to validate the proposed framework, we present experiment results and analysis obtained from the user study where 30 human subjects participated and simulated robot experiments.

Title: Towards Automatic Migration of ROS Components from Software to Hardware.
Authors: Anders Blaabjerg Lange & Ulrik Pagh Schultz & Anders Stengaard Sørensen
Abstract: The use of the ROS middleware is a growing trend in robotics in general, ROS and hard real-time embedded systems have however not been easily uniteable while retaining the same overall communication and processing methodology at all levels. In this paper we present an approach aimed at tackling the schism between high-level, flexible software and low-level, real-time software. The key idea of our approach is to enable software components written for a high-level publish-subscribe software architecture to be automatically migrated to a dedicated hardware architecture implemented using programmable logic.

Title: SOTER on ROS: A Run-Time Assurance Framework on the Robot Operating System.
Authors: Sumukh Shivakumar & Hazem Torfah & Ankush Desai & Sanjit A. Seshia
Abstract: Ensuring the safety and reliability of autonomous robots is a critical challenge. This paper presents SOTER on ROS, a run-time assurance framework integrated with the Robot Operating System (ROS). SOTER provides a mechanism for monitoring the robot's behavior and ensuring that it adheres to predefined safety properties. If a property is violated, the framework can trigger corrective actions, such as switching to a safe control mode. We describe the architecture of SOTER and its implementation in ROS, and demonstrate its effectiveness through a case study involving a mobile robot in a dynamic environment.

Title: A ROS multi-ontology references services: OWL reasoners and application prototyping issues.
Authors: Luca Buoncompagni & Alessio Capitanelli & Fulvio Mastrogiovanni
Abstract: Developing complex robotic applications often requires the integration of knowledge from multiple domains. This paper proposes a ROS multi-ontology reference service that enables the use of OWL reasoners within a ROS-based system. The service provides a standardized interface for accessing and reasoning over multiple ontologies, facilitating the development of intelligent, knowledge-driven behaviors. We discuss the application prototyping issues associated with this approach and demonstrate its utility through several examples, showing how it simplifies the management and use of diverse knowledge sources in robotics.

Title: Integrating Soft Robotics with ROS - A hybrid pick and place arm.
Authors: Ross M. McKenzie & Thomas W. Barraclough & Adam A. Stokes
Abstract: Soft robotics offers new possibilities for robots to interact with their environment in a safe and flexible manner. This paper describes the integration of soft robotics with the Robot Operating System (ROS) through the development of a hybrid pick-and-place arm. The arm combines soft and rigid components, leveraging the strengths of both. We describe the design and control of the arm, including its ROS-based software architecture. Experimental results show that the hybrid arm can perform a variety of pick-and-place tasks, demonstrating the benefits of integrating soft robotics with established robotic software frameworks.

Title: ROSY: An elegant language to teach the pure reactive nature of robot programming.
Authors: Hugo Pacheco 0001 & Nuno Macedo
Abstract: Teaching robot programming to novices is a challenging task, as it requires balancing the need for expressive power with the ease of use. This paper introduces ROSY, an elegant language designed to teach the pure reactive nature of robot programming. ROSY provides a high-level, declarative syntax for specifying robot behaviors based on sensor inputs and actuator commands. It is built on top of the Robot Operating System (ROS), allowing students to easily deploy their programs on real or simulated robots. We describe the design principles of ROSY and discuss its use in an introductory robotics course.

Title: Robot_gym: accelerated robot training through simulation in the cloud with ROS and Gazebo.
Authors: Victor Mayoral Vilches & Alejandro Hernández Cordero & Asier Bilbao Calvo & Irati Zamalloa Ugarte & Risto Kojcev
Abstract: Rather than programming, training allows robots to achieve behaviors that generalize better and are capable to respond to real-world needs. However, such training requires a big amount of experimentation which is not always feasible for a physical robot. In this work, we present robot_gym, a framework to accelerate robot training through simulation in the cloud that makes use of roboticists' tools, simplifying the development and deployment processes on real robots. We unveil that, for simple tasks, simple 3DoF robots require more than 140 attempts to learn. For more complex, 6DoF robots, the number of attempts increases to more than 900 for the same task. We demonstrate that our framework, for simple tasks, accelerates the robot training time by more than 33% while maintaining similar levels of accuracy and repeatability.

Title: ROSIE: Runtime Optimization of SPARQL Queries Using Incremental Evaluation.
Authors: Lei Gai & Wei Chen 0021 & Tengjiao Wang 0003
Abstract: Querying large-scale semantic data in real-time is a key requirement for many intelligent systems. This paper presents ROSIE, a framework for the runtime optimization of SPARQL queries using incremental evaluation. ROSIE is designed to work within the Robot Operating System (ROS), providing a powerful and efficient way for robots to reason over semantic streams. We describe the optimization techniques used in ROSIE and demonstrate its performance through a series of benchmarks, showing that it significantly reduces query evaluation time compared to traditional approaches.

Title: TabulaROSA: Tabular Operating System Architecture for Massively Parallel Heterogeneous Compute Engines.
Authors: Jeremy Kepner & Ron Brightwell & Alan Edelman & Vijay Gadepally & Hayden Jananthan & Michael Jones 0001 & Sam Madden & Peter Michaleas & Hamed Okhravi & Kevin T. Pedretti & Albert Reuther & Thomas L. Sterling & Mike Stonebraker
Abstract: Massively parallel heterogeneous compute engines offer great potential for improving the performance of complex robotic algorithms. This paper introduces TabulaROSA, a tabular operating system architecture designed for such engines. TabulaROSA provides a unified interface for managing and scheduling tasks across a variety of hardware accelerators, facilitating the development of high-performance robotic applications. We describe the design and implementation of TabulaROSA and discuss its integration with the Robot Operating System (ROS), showing how it can be used to accelerate key robotic tasks.

Title: ChoiRbot: A ROS 2 Toolbox for Cooperative Robotics.
Authors: Andrea Testa & Andrea Camisa & Giuseppe Notarstefano
Abstract: ChoiRbot is a software framework designed for distributed multi-robot systems, providing a modular toolset for executing complex, distributed tasks in networks of heterogeneous robots without a central coordinator. Built on ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2), it leverages native support for distributed communication. The toolbox uses a three-layer structure consisting of a Team Guidance Layer for high-level decision-making, a RoboPlanning Layer for task planning, and a RoboControl Layer for low-level control. It is specifically optimized for distributed optimization-based control schemes like MPC and task assignment.

Title: Closed loop interactions between spiking neural network and robotic simulators based on MUSIC and ROS.
Authors: Philipp Weidel & Mikael Djurfeldt & Renato Carlos Farinha Duarte & Abigail Morrison
Abstract: Closed-loop interactions between spiking neural networks (SNNs) and robotic simulators are essential for research in neuromorphic robotics. This paper presents a framework for such interactions based on the integration of MUSIC and the Robot Operating System (ROS). The framework allows for the bidirectional exchange of information between an SNN, simulated in a neuromorphic simulator, and a robot, simulated in a ROS-compatible simulator. We describe the design and implementation of the framework and demonstrate its utility through several examples, showing its potential for developing and testing biologically-inspired robotic controllers.

Title: Rapid Bipedal Gait Design Using C-FROST with Illustration on a Cassie-series Robot.
Authors: Ayonga Hereid & Omar Harib & Ross Hartley & Yukai Gong & Jessy W. Grizzle
Abstract: One of the big attractions of low-dimensional models for gait design has been the ability to compute solutions rapidly, whereas one of their drawbacks has been the difficulty in mapping the solutions back to the target robot. This paper presents a set of tools for rapidly determining solutions for 'humanoids' without removing or lumping degrees of freedom. The main tools are: (1) C-FROST, an open-source C++ interface for FROST, a direct collocation optimization tool; and (2) multi-threading. The efficiency of C-FROST is illustrated by designing a library of different periodic walking gaits for the Cassie-series bipedal robot.

Title: ROSoClingo: A ROS package for ASP-based robot control.
Authors: Benjamin Andres & Philipp Obermeier & Orkunt Sabuncu & Torsten Schaub & David Rajaratnam
Abstract: Knowledge representation and reasoning capacities are vital to cognitive robotics because they provide higher level cognitive functions for reasoning about actions, environments, goals, perception, etc. Although Answer Set Programming (ASP) is well suited for modelling such functions, there was so far no seamless way to use ASP in a robotic environment. We address this shortcoming and show how a recently developed reactive ASP system can be harnessed to provide appropriate reasoning capacities within a robotic system. We furnish a ROS package integrating the reactive ASP solver oClingo with the popular open-source ROS robotic middleware. The resulting system, called ROSoClingo, provides a generic method by which an ASP program can be used to control the behavior of a robot.

Title: Security and Performance Considerations in ROS 2: A Balancing Act.
Authors: Jongkil Kim & Jonathon M. Smereka & Calvin Cheung & Surya Nepal & Marthie Grobler
Abstract: Robot Operating System (ROS) 2 is a ground-up re-design of ROS 1 to support performance critical cyber-physical systems (CPSs) using the Data Distribution Service (DDS) middleware. Accordingly, the security of ROS 2 is highly reliant on the security of its DDS communication protocol. However, finding a balance between the performance and security is non-trivial task. Inappropriate security implementations may cause not only significant loss on performance of the system, but also security failures in the system. In this paper, we provide an analysis of the DDS security protocol as well as an overview on how to find the balance between performance and security. To accomplish this, we evaluate the latency and throughput of the communication protocols of ROS 2 in both wired and wireless networks, and measure the efficiency loss caused by the enabling of security protocols such as Virtual Private Network (VPN) and DDS security protocol in ROS 2 in both network setups. The result can be directly used by robotics developers to find the optimal and balanced settings of ROS 2 applications. Additionally, we analyzed the security specification of DDS using existing security standards and tested the implementation of the DDS protocol by performing static analysis. The results of this work can be used to enhance the security of ROS 2.

Title: Utilizing ROS 1 and the Turtlebot3 in a Multi-Robot System.
Authors: Corey Williams & Adam Schroeder
Abstract: ROS (Robot Operating System) has become ubiquitous for testing new algorithms, alternative hardware configurations, and prototyping. By performing research with its modular framework, it can streamline sharing new work and integrations. However, it has many features and new terms that can take a considerable amount of time to learn for a new user. This paper will explore how to set up and configure ROS and ROS packages to work with a multi-robot system on a single master network.

Title: ROSA: Robust sparse adaptive channel estimation in the presence of impulsive noises.
Authors: Guan Gui & Li Xu 0004 & Nobuhiro Shimoi
Abstract: This paper proposes three sparse variable step-size affine projection sign algorithms (VSS-APSA) to take advantage of channel sparsity under different sparse constraints, specifically considering alpha-stable (impulsive) noise. Theoretical analysis and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed correntropy-inspired variable step-size method for the sign algorithm is computationally simple and robust to impulsive noises. The approach improves convergence speed and tracking capability while maintaining steady-state performance in non-Gaussian noise environments.

Title: ROS Based Visual Programming Tool for Mobile Robot Education and Applications.
Authors: Mustafa Karaca & Ugur Yayan
Abstract: Visual programming languages (VPLs) provide coding without typing texts. VPL makes coding easy to programmers with automatically adding usually used some code structure. Syntax errors are impossible with VPLs because of there is no forgotten parentheses and semicolons. VPLs provide to focus on algorithm for programmers. VPL is a new trend for educational robotic environments. In this study, Robot Operating System (ROS) compatible web based visual programming system has been developed for evarobot. Blockly has been used as VPL for the study and to generate / use blocks (communication, sensing etc.). Some applications were generated like teleoperation, SLAM and wander etc. In this system, communication between server and client is supported by rosbridge package. Web page connected to ROS which runs on server using roslibjs library.

Title: ROSE: A Retinal OCT-Angiography Vessel Segmentation Dataset and New Model.
Authors: Yuhui Ma & Huaying Hao & Huazhu Fu & Jiong Zhang & Jianlong Yang & Jiang Liu 0001 & Yalin Zheng & Yitian Zhao
Abstract: Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA) is a non-invasive imaging technique that has been increasingly used to image the retinal vasculature at capillary level resolution. However, automated segmentation of retinal vessels in OCTA has been under-studied due to various challenges such as low capillary visibility and high vessel complexity. To address these issues, for the first time in the field of retinal image analysis we construct a dedicated Retinal OCTA SEgmentation dataset (ROSE), which consists of 229 OCTA images with vessel annotations at either centerline-level or pixel level. Secondly, we introduce a novel split-based coarse-to-fine vessel segmentation network for OCTA images (OCTA-Net), with the ability to detect thick and thin vessels separately. The experimental results demonstrate that our OCTA-Net yields better vessel segmentation performance in OCTA than both traditional and other deep learning methods.

Title: ROS and Buzz: consensus-based behaviors for heterogeneous teams.
Authors: David St-Onge & Vivek Shankar Varadharajan & Guannan Li & Ivan Svogor & Giovanni Beltrame
Abstract: This paper presents ROS and Buzz, a framework for consensus-based behaviors for heterogeneous teams of robots. The integration of the Robot Operating System (ROS) with the Buzz programming language allows for the development of swarm behaviors that are scalable, verifiable, and easy to deploy. We describe the software architecture and the communication primitives used to achieve consensus among robots with different capabilities. The framework is validated through simulations and experiments with a heterogeneous team of ground and aerial robots, demonstrating its effectiveness for tasks such as collective area coverage and target tracking.

Title: Extending the OpenAI Gym for robotics: a toolkit for reinforcement learning using ROS and Gazebo.
Authors: Iker Zamora & Nestor Gonzalez Lopez & Victor Mayoral Vilches & Alejandro Hernández Cordero
Abstract: This paper presents an extension of the OpenAI Gym for robotics using the Robot Operating System (ROS) and the Gazebo simulator. The content discusses the software architecture proposed and the results obtained by using two Reinforcement Learning techniques: Q-Learning and Sarsa. Ultimately, the output of this work presents a benchmarking system for robotics that allows different techniques and algorithms to be compared using the same virtual conditions.

Title: ira_laser_tools: a ROS LaserScan manipulation toolbox.
Authors: Augusto Luis Ballardini & Simone Fontana & Axel Furlan & Domenico G. Sorrenti
Abstract: Laser scanners are sensors of widespread use in robotic applications. Under the Robot Operating System (ROS) the information generated by laser scanners can be conveyed by either LaserScan messages or in the form of PointClouds. Many publicly available algorithms (mapping, localization, navigation, etc.) rely on LaserScan messages, yet a tool for handling multiple lasers, merging their measurements, or to generate generic LaserScan messages from PointClouds, is not available. This report describes two tools, in the form of ROS nodes, which we release as open source under the BSD license, which allow to either merge multiple single-plane laser scans or to generate virtual laser scans from a point cloud. A short tutorial, along with the main advantages and limitations of these tools are presented.

Title: ROSA Analyser: An automatized approach to analyse processes of ROSA
Authors: Raúl Pardo & Fernando L. Pelayo
Abstract:
In this work we present the first version of ROSA Analyser, a tool designed to get closer to a fully automatic process of analysing the behaviour of a system specified as a process of the Markovian Process Algebra ROSA. In this first development stage, ROSA Analyser is able to generate the Labelled Transition System, according to ROSA Operational Semantics. 
ROSA Analyser performance starts with the Syntactic Analysis so generating a layered structure, suitable to then, apply the Operational Semantics Transition rules in the easier way. ROSA Analyser is able to recognize some states identities deeper than the Syntactic ones. This is the very first step in the way to reduce the size of the LTS and then to avoid the state explosion problem, so making this task more tractable. 
For the sake of better illustrating the usefulness of ROSA Analyser, a case study is also provided within this work.

Title: Towards Live Programming in ROS with PhaROS and LRP.
Authors: Pablo Estefo & Miguel Campusano & Luc Fabresse & Johan Fabry & Jannik Laval & Noury Bouraqadi
Abstract: Live programming offers the potential to significantly improve the development process for robotic systems by providing immediate feedback on program behavior. This paper presents an approach toward live programming in ROS using PhaROS and the Live Robot Programming (LRP) language. PhaROS is a ROS client for the Pharo Smalltalk environment, and LRP is a language for specifying robot behaviors as nested state machines. We describe the integration of these tools and demonstrate how they enable the live creation and modification of ROS-based robot behaviors without the need for manual compilation and restart cycles.

Title: CROSS-MBCR: Exact Minimum Bandwith Coordinated Regenerating Codes
Authors: Steve Jiekak & Nicolas Le Scouarnec
Abstract: We study the exact and optimal repair of multiple failures in codes for distributed storage. More particularly, we provide an explicit construction of exact minimum bandwidth coordinated regenerating codes (MBCR) for n = d + t, k, d >= k, t >= 1. Our construction differs from existing constructions by allowing both t > 1 (i.e., repair of multiple failures) and d > k (i.e., contacting more than k devices during repair). We focus on this problem and propose an explicit code construction for the case of n = d + t, k, d >= k, t >= 1 thus relaxing the constraint d = k present in previous constructions.

Title: robROSE: A robust approach for dealing with imbalanced data in fraud detection.
Authors: Bart Baesens & Sebastiaan Höppner & Irene Ortner & Tim Verdonck
Abstract: Fraud detection is a critical task in many financial systems, but it is often hampered by the problem of imbalanced data, where the number of fraudulent transactions is much smaller than the number of legitimate ones. This paper introduces robROSE, a robust approach for dealing with imbalanced data in fraud detection. robROSE combines the Robust Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (ROSE) with advanced feature selection and ensemble learning methods to improve the detection of fraudulent transactions while minimizing false positives. We evaluate the performance of robROSE on several real-world datasets and show that it significantly outperforms existing methods, particularly in scenarios with high levels of imbalance and noise.

Title: CROSSLINE: Breaking "Security-by-Crash" based Memory Isolation in AMD SEV.
Authors: Mengyuan Li & Yinqian Zhang & Zhiqiang Lin
Abstract: AMD's Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV) is an emerging security feature of modern AMD processors that allows virtual machines to run with encrypted memory and perform confidential computing even with an untrusted hypervisor. This paper first demystifies SEV's improper use of address space identifier (ASID) for controlling accesses of a VM to encrypted memory pages, cache lines, and TLB entries. We then present the CROSSLINE attacks, a novel class of attacks against SEV that allow the adversary to launch an attacker VM and change its ASID to that of the victim VM to impersonate the victim. We present two variants of CROSSLINE attacks: CROSSLINE V1 decrypts victim's page tables or any memory blocks conforming to the format of a page table entry; CROSSLINE V2 constructs encryption and decryption oracles by executing instructions of the victim VM. We discuss the applicability of CROSSLINE attacks on AMD's SEV, SEV-ES, and SEV-SNP processors.

Title: iviz: A ROS Visualization App for Mobile Devices.
Authors: Antonio Zea 0001 & Uwe D. Hanebeck
Abstract: iviz is a mobile application for the visualization and control of robots running the Robot Operating System (ROS). It provides a high-performance, cross-platform interface for visualizing ROS data, including 3D sensor data, robot models, and navigation maps. Built using the Unity game engine, iviz offers a user-friendly and interactive way to monitor and interact with ROS-enabled systems from mobile devices. We describe the features and architecture of iviz and discuss its use in several robotics research and education scenarios.

Title: ROS open-source audio recognizer: ROAR environmental sound detection tools for robot programming.
Authors: Joseph M. Romano & Jordan Brindza & Katherine J. Kuchenbecker
Abstract: Surprisingly, very few autonomous robots leverage audio feedback to improve their performance. Modern audio recognition techniques exist that are capable of learning and recognizing real-world sounds, but few implementations exist that are easily incorporated into modern robotic programming frameworks. This paper presents a new software library known as the ROS Open-source Audio Recognizer (ROAR). ROAR provides a complete set of end-to-end tools for online supervised learning of new audio events, feature extraction, automatic one-class Support Vector Machine model tuning, and real-time audio event detection.

Title: A direct visual servoing-based framework for the 2016 IROS Autonomous Drone Racing Challenge.
Authors: Sunggoo Jung & Sungwook Cho & Dasol Lee & Hanseob Lee & David Hyunchul Shim
Abstract: Abstract This paper presents a framework for navigating in obstacle‐dense environments as posed in the 2016 International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) Autonomous Drone Racing Challenge. Our framework is based on direct visual servoing and leg‐by‐leg planning to navigate in a complex environment filled with many similar frame‐shaped obstacles to fly through. Our indoor navigation method relies on the velocity measurement by an optical flow sensor since the position measurements from GPS or external cameras are not available. For precision navigation through a sequence of obstacles, a center point–matching method is used with the depth information from the onboard stereo camera. The guidance points is directly generated in three‐dimensional space using the two‐dimensional image data to avoid accumulating the error from the sensor drift. The proposed framework is implemented on a quadrotor‐based aerial vehicle, which carries an onboard vision‐processing computer for self‐contained operation. Using the proposed method, our drone was able to finished in first place in the world‐premier IROS Autonomous Drone Racing Challenge.

Title: Hello robot can you come here?: using ROS4iOS to provide remote perceptual capabilities for visual location; speech and speaker recognition.
Authors: François Ferland & Ronan Chauvin & Dominic Létourneau & François Michaud
Abstract: Mobile devices such as smartphones and tablets can provide additional sensing and interacting capabilities to a mobile robot, even extending its senses to remote locations. To do so, we developed ROS4iOS, a native port of ROS allowing to seamlessly use data from mobile iOS devices to be processed on a robot. To demonstrate this capability, this video presentation illustrates how ROS4iOS has been used to implement an assistance scenario: a person in a remote location asks our IRL-1 robot for assistance, and IRL-1 must recognize the person's voice, identify the remote location using images taken from the mobile device, navigate to the identified location and interact vocally with the person through the mobile device.

Title: ROS and Rosbridge: roboticists out of the loop.
Authors: Christopher Crick & Graylin Jay & Sarah Osentoski & Odest Chadwicke Jenkins
Abstract: The Robot Operating System (ROS) has become a standard for robotics research, providing a common platform for software development and integration. However, ROS is primarily designed for use by roboticists, and its complexity can be a barrier for researchers in other fields, such as human-computer interaction (HCI) and psychology. In this paper, we present rosbridge, a lightweight, language-independent interface to ROS that allows non-roboticists to interact with ROS-enabled robots using standard web technologies. Rosbridge provides a simple JSON-based API for subscribing to and publishing ROS topics, calling ROS services, and managing ROS parameters. We describe the design and implementation of rosbridge and demonstrate its utility through several case studies. Our results show that rosbridge significantly reduces the barrier to entry for non-roboticists, enabling them to easily integrate robots into their research and applications.

Title: Facial Expression Synthesis on Robots: An ROS Module.
Authors: Maryam Moosaei & Cory J. Hayes & Laurel D. Riek
Abstract: We present a generalized technique for easily synthesizing facial expressions on robotic faces. In contrast to other work, our approach works in near real time with a high level of synchrony. We described a generalized method for facial expression synthesis on robots, and its implementation in an open-source, end-to-end ROS module. In one validation implementation, CLM-based performance-driven synthesis, our method can accurately map both live and recorded performers to a 16-F-DOF robotic face. The module is easily extensible to multiple platforms, regardless of the sensor, feature processor, translator, or control interface.

Title: AEROSTABILES: A new approach to HRI researches.
Authors: David St-Onge & Nicolas Reeves & Philippe Giguère & Inna Sharf & Gregory Dudek & Ioannis M. Rekleitis & Pierre-Yves Brèches & Patrick Abouzakhm & Philippe Babin
Abstract: This paper presents a research-creation project involving large-scale, lighter-than-air (LTA) robotic structures designed for human-robot interaction (HRI). The 'Aerostabiles' are autonomous or semi-autonomous flying sculptures that use their physical presence and movement to interact with humans in public spaces. The research focuses on the challenges of controlling large, flexible structures and explores how their organic, slow-moving nature affects human perception and social interaction compared to traditional rigid robots.

Title: Tether tracking and control of ROSA robotic rover.
Authors: Jamshed Iqbal & Seppo Heikkilä & Aarne Halme
Abstract: Mars is currently the centre of interest for space exploration. Rovers are major sources of information of Mars. This paper focuses on a robotic roving vehicle robot for scientific applications (ROSA) having its initiative of European Space Agency (ESA). ROSA being a tethered rover makes possible to reduce the weight/size of rover because power is delivered through tether. Additionally, tether provides communication lines between the lander and the rover. Tethered robots require complex control system. This paper presents a specialized tracking system for rover's 40 m tether. The proposed system is capable of tether tracking within resolution of +-6 cm.

Title: Comparing Robot Grasping Teleoperation Across Desktop and Virtual Reality with ROS Reality.
Authors: David Whitney & Eric Rosen & Elizabeth Phillips & George Dimitri Konidaris & Stefanie Tellex
Abstract: Teleoperation is a key capability for many robotic applications, but it can be challenging for users to perform complex tasks such as grasping. This paper compares robot grasping teleoperation across desktop and virtual reality (VR) interfaces using ROS Reality, a framework for linking ROS-enabled robots with VR environments. We evaluate the performance of users in a series of grasping tasks, comparing completion time and success rate between the two interface types. Our results show that VR-based teleoperation can provide significant advantages in terms of spatial awareness and task efficiency, particularly for users with limited experience in robot control.

Title: A ROS framework for the extrinsic calibration of intelligent vehicles: A multi-sensor; multi-modal approach.
Authors: Miguel Oliveira & Afonso Castro & Tiago Madeira & Eurico F. Pedrosa & Paulo Dias & Vítor M. F. Santos
Abstract: Abstract This paper proposes a general approach to the problem of extrinsic calibration of multiple sensors of varied modalities. This is of particular relevance for intelligent vehicles, which are complex systems that often encompass several sensors of different modalities. Our approach is seamlessly integrated with the Robot Operating System (ROS) framework, and allows for the interactive positioning of sensors and labelling of data, facilitating the calibration procedure. The calibration is formulated as a simultaneous optimization for all sensors, in which the objective function accounts for the various sensor modalities. Results show that the proposed procedure produces accurate calibrations, on par with state of the art approaches which operate only for pairwise setups.

Title: iCab Use Case for ROS-based Architecture.
Authors: Pablo Marín-Plaza & Ahmed Hussein & David Martín 0001 & Arturo de la Escalera
Abstract: Abstract In the Intelligent Transportation Systems Community, the research interest in intelligent and autonomous vehicles is increasing during the last few years. Accordingly, this paper presents the advances in the development of a ROS-based (Robot Operating System) software architecture for intelligent vehicles. The main contribution of the architecture is its powerfulness (in the aspect of complete architecture managing the navigation, synchronization, communication, cooperation, ground station, web server, sound system), flexibility (due to the fact that autonomous vehicles evolve exponentially where new sensors or algorithms emerge every day), and modularity (in the form of interchangeability and replaceability for new modules), which allow researchers to develop and test different algorithms easily and faster. The architecture has been tested on two real platforms such as the iCab (Intelligent Campus Automobile) and IvvI 2.0 vehicle (Intelligent Vehicle based on Visual Information) The first one is a full y autonomous vehicle and the second one provides Advance Driver Assistance. Additionally, it is shown the use case for the iCab project where the scope is focused on the critical element of navigation. The use case exposed proves the benefits of using this architecture over a centralized one.

Title: Pound: A multi-master ROS node for reducing delay and jitter in wireless multi-robot networks.
Authors: Danilo Tardioli & Ramviyas Parasuraman & Petter Ögren
Abstract: Abstract The Robot Operating System (ROS) is a popular and widely used software framework for building robotics systems. With the growth of its popularity, it has started to be used in multi-robot systems as well. However, the TCP connections that the platform relies on for connecting the so-called ROS nodes presents several issues regarding limited-bandwidth, delays, and jitter, when used in wireless multi-hop networks. In this paper, we present a thorough analysis of the problem and propose a new ROS node called Pound to improve the wireless communication performance by reducing delay and jitter in data exchanges, especially in multi-hop networks. Pound allows the use of multiple ROS masters (roscores), features data compression, and importantly, introduces a priority scheme that allows favoring more important flows over less important ones. We compare Pound to the state-of-the-art solutions through extensive experiments and show that it performs equally well, or better in all the test cases, including a control-over-network example.

Title: Development and Experimental Tests of a ROS Multi-agent Structure for Autonomous Surface Vehicles.
Authors: Giuseppe Conte & David Scaradozzi & Daniele Mannocchi & Paolo Raspa & Luca Panebianco & Laura Screpanti
Abstract: Robotic structures that couple autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) and unmanned underwater vehicles (UUVs) in integrated systems with various levels of cooperation provide interesting solutions for exploration, monitoring and exploitation of the underwater environment. In this paper, we describe the development and preliminary field testing of an autonomous surface vehicle that can automatically track, deploy and recover a small remotely operated vehicle (ROV), which is guided from a shore-ground station. This goal is achieved by exploiting a ROS-based multi-agent architecture.

Title: Integrating Arduino-Based Educational Mobile Robots in ROS.
Authors: André G. Araújo & David Portugal & Micael S. Couceiro & Rui P. Rocha
Abstract: Arduino and ROS are two popular open-source platforms for robotics, but integrating them can be challenging due to their different communication protocols and processing capabilities. This paper describes the design and implementation of a library for integrating Arduino-based educational mobile robots into the ROS ecosystem. The library provides a simple and efficient way for Arduino-based robots to communicate with ROS-enabled systems over a variety of serial and wireless interfaces. We demonstrate the utility of the library through several educational scenarios, showing how it enables students to leverage the power of ROS for high-level tasks such as mapping and navigation on low-cost Arduino platforms.

Title: ROS open-source audio recognizer: ROAR environmental sound detection tools for robot programming.
Authors: Joseph M. Romano & Jordan Brindza & Katherine J. Kuchenbecker
Abstract: Surprisingly, very few autonomous robots leverage audio feedback to improve their performance. Modern audio recognition techniques exist that are capable of learning and recognizing real-world sounds, but few implementations exist that are easily incorporated into modern robotic programming frameworks. This paper presents a new software library known as the ROS Open-source Audio Recognizer (ROAR). ROAR provides a complete set of end-to-end tools for online supervised learning of new audio events, feature extraction, automatic one-class Support Vector Machine model tuning, and real-time audio event detection.
